coredata.dc:title: Facile assembly of effective carbon quantum dots and multiwall carbon nanotubes supported MnO2 hybrid nanoparticles for enhanced photocatalytic and anticancer activity
coredata.dc:creator: Bhuvaneswari, K.
Sreeja, B.S.
Radha, S.
Saranya, J.
Palanisamy, G.
Srinivasan, M.
Pazhanivel, T.
coredata.dc:description: The multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) supported MnO2 hybrid nanoparticles were prepared by simple hydrothermal approach. Photocatalytic degradation and anticancer activity of the as-prepared hybrid samples were investigated against the methylene blue (MB) dye and HeP2 cell respectively. The hybrid nanoparticles show improved photocatalytic degradation against MB dye due to its fast charge carrier generation and improved charge carrier mobility. Similarly, the photocatalytic degradation ability of the 0.1 weight percentage of MWCNT loaded MnO2/CQDs hybrid samples show improved photocatalytic activity compared to other samples. In addition, the as-prepared MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-1, and MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2 hybrid nanocomposites were assessed for their scavenging anti-cancer ability against HeLa cell lines in the different doses at various incubation intervals. In anticancer activity against HeLa cell lines, the high weight percentage of chitosan derived CQDs supported MWCNT/MnO2 hybrid samples shows a high inhibition rate at minimal concentration due to their good synergy through effective molecular bindings. Also, the observed effective IC50 concentration of the MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2 exhibits 15.6 μg/mL. All these results affirm that CQDs derived from chitosan play a significant role in comparison with MWCNT in achieving 50 % cell viability at minimum IC50 concentration.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Anti-cancer activity
Carbon quantum dots
In-vitro cytotoxicity
Photocatalytic degradation
MWCNT
MnO2

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Anticancer effect of MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-1 on HeP2 cell line
Anticancer effect of MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2 on HeP2 cell line

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-) HRTEM images of MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2 (f) SAED pattern of MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2 and (h) Histograms for particle size distribution of CQD.
XRD patterns of (a) MnO2, (b) MWCNT, (c) Chitosan (d) MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-1, and (e) MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2.
FTIR spectra for (a) MnO2, (b) MWCNT, (c) Chitosan (d) MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-1, and (e) MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2.
(i) Plot of C/ C0 curve and (ii) Pseudo first order rate kinetics of (a) MnO2, (b) MWCNT, (c) Chitosan (d) MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-1 and (e) MWCNT/CQD/MnO2 −2.
(i) Radical trapping study and (ii) cyclic stability of MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2 against MB dye.
Schematic demonstration for degradation mechanism of MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2 hybrid photocatalysis.
% Cell Viability of MWCNT/CQD/MnO2 −1 and MWCNT/CQD/MnO2 −2 on HeP2 cell Line.
MTT assay result of (a) MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-1 and (b) MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2.
Anticancer effect of MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-1 on HeP2 Cell line (a) Normal Hep2 Cell line (b) Toxicity −1000 µg/ml (c) Toxicity −250 µg/ (d) Toxicity −62.5 µg/ml (e) Toxicity −15.6 µg/ml.
Anticancer effect of MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2 on HeP2 Cell line (a) Normal Hep2 Cell line (b) Toxicity −1000 µg/ml (c) Toxicity −250 µg/ml (d) Toxicity 62.5 µg/ml (e) Toxicity −15.6 µg/ml.
In-vitro cytotoxicity evaluation of MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-1 and MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2 systems on HeP2 cell Line.
Comparative study of as prepared sample with various nanoparticles against different cell lines.

row.entry: Gold NPs mediated by peel extract of Annona Muricata, Soursop fruit
Chitosan derived CQD anchored MnO2@ MWCNT

head.ce:title: Facile assembly of effective carbon quantum dots and multiwall carbon nanotubes supported MnO2 hybrid nanoparticles for enhanced photocatalytic and anticancer activity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Smart Materials Laboratory, Department of Physics, Periyar University, Salem 636 011, Tamilnadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) supported MnO2 hybrid nanoparticles were prepared by simple hydrothermal approach. Photocatalytic degradation and anticancer activity of the as-prepared hybrid samples were investigated against the methylene blue (MB) dye and HeP2 cell respectively. The hybrid nanoparticles show improved photocatalytic degradation against MB dye due to its fast charge carrier generation and improved charge carrier mobility. Similarly, the photocatalytic degradation ability of the 0.1 weight percentage of MWCNT loaded MnO2/CQDs hybrid samples show improved photocatalytic activity compared to other samples. In addition, the as-prepared MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-1, and MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2 hybrid nanocomposites were assessed for their scavenging anti-cancer ability against HeLa cell lines in the different doses at various incubation intervals. In anticancer activity against HeLa cell lines, the high weight percentage of chitosan derived CQDs supported MWCNT/MnO2 hybrid samples shows a high inhibition rate at minimal concentration due to their good synergy through effective molecular bindings. Also, the observed effective IC50 concentration of the MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2 exhibits 15.6 μg/mL. All these results affirm that CQDs derived from chitosan play a significant role in comparison with MWCNT in achieving 50 % cell viability at minimum IC50 concentration.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental pollution has focused a major issue for modern civilization, it mostly poses a direct challenge to the survival of human beings and other living organisms. Particularly the impact of water pollution causes serious health effects on human beings [1]. Approximately 700,000 tons of dyes are developed each year around the world. The fabric dyes account for marginally more than half of this production, with the remaining 15 % of dyes used for other substrates including leather and paper [2]. Particularly, dye-containing effluents are known as an important source of environmental pollution which were causing toxic and carcinogenic effects [3-5]. Since the degradation of organic pollutants through the photocatalytic process can provide a convenient way to use light to conduct chemical transformations and this method has drawn much attention to many of the modern world’s environmental challenges [6]. Semiconducting materials based on photocatalysis degradation of organic molecules have been confirmed to be a hopeful method to purify wastewater. In general, the TiO2 and ZnO photocatalysts have been established as the important material in the degradation of pollutants from water by photocatalytic process owing to their exclusive and extensive properties such as a large free-exciton binding energy, wide energy band gap in the near UV region and high oxidation capability [7-13]. However, the major drawbacks of commercial photocatalyst materials are the low absorption of visible light and high band gap energy restricting the generation of photo-induced charge carriers. Because the utilization of UV light illumination restricts the transition of fundamental research toward the practical applications [14,15].

In the past few decades, researchers have been looking for photocatalysts that are driven by visible light. Hence, numerous approaches have been planned to develop visible light active photocatalysts for the better use of sunlight irradiation [16,17]. In recent years, manganese oxides (MnO2) are attractive in a various emerging field such as adsorption, battery, sensors and supercapacitor [18-20]. Among them photocatalytic activity has attracted much attention due to its high reserves, high reserves, low toxicity, low cost, strong adsorption, oxidation ability eco-friendly, earth-abundant, as well as narrow energy band gap [21,22]. MnO2 belongs to the diverse categories of materials that have fundamental and technological significance. Also, the photocatalytic degradation ability of the MnO2 is mostly associated with the high surface area, morphology, and tends to display improved photocatalytic activity [23]. The low charge carrier mobility and fast recombination rate of photo-generated charge carriers which are limits the usage and photocatalytic activity of MnO2. Numerous effects have been proposed to increase the photocatalytic activity of MnO2 like the incorporation of a supporting co-catalyst to increase the mobility and lifetime of charge carriers which results in high photocatalytic activity [19]. Among the various approaches like introduction of dopants, other metal oxides, polymers and zeolites materials coupling of carbon-based materials know as a valid approach to increase the lifetime of the photo-generated charge carriers [24-26]. Carbon nanomaterials like graphene, graphene oxide, reduced graphene oxide carbon nanotubes, carbon aerogel and quantum dots have been widely investigated with traditional photocatalytic material to achieve high degradation efficiency [27-30]. Among them, multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) have widely used as promising co-catalyst with traditional photocatalyst materials due to their excellent electrical, morphological and physical properties [11,31,32]. Also, the numerous defects and irregularities on the surface of MWCNTs can assist as trap states for the photo-generated electrons [33]. Similarly, the exclusive features of MWCNT, such as their conductivity and electron-accepting ability can improve the recombination rate of photo-generated charge carriers. Recently Badr A. El-Sayed et al. prepared MWCNTs/TiO2 nanocomposites for effective removal of Dianix Blue and Vat Green 1 Dyes [33] through a photocatalytic process. Also, CQDs exhibited both up and down conversion fluorescence which worked as electron reservoirs in the photocatalytic activity. Tao Shen et. al prepared carbon QD/TiO2 heterojunctions by hydrothermal method and this heterojunction formation effectively degrade the phenol under visible light irradiation [34].

In this work, different weight percentages of CQDs and MWCNT-supported MnO2 hybrid nanoparticles were synthesized by a hydrothermal process. The morphological, structural, optical, and elemental studies of the prepared samples were examined by various analytical methods. The photocatalytic degradation ability of the prepared samples was examined against the methylene blue (MB) dye under UV–visible light irradiation. The major motive of this work is to examine the role of MWCNT and CQDs in photocatalytic degradation and anticancer activity by optimizing their weight percentages, and the observed results have been discussed in detail.

The supporting information file contains detailed preparation and characterization instructions for MnO2, CQDs, MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-1 and MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2. Fig. 1 shows HRTEM images and Selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of the MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-1 nanomaterial. Morphological study reveals that the CQD and MWCNT assisted MnO2 nanoparticles were developed successfully. Fig. 1 (a and b) shows the dispersion of CQD, MnO2 and MWCNT. Zero-dimensional carbon QD is well dispersed over the surface of MWCNTs and MnO2 nanorods. There seems to be evidence in Fig. 1(a and b) that some MnO2 and CQD nanoparticles attached to MWCNTs, as well as MnO2 nanoparticles loaded with CQD, were attached to MWCNTs (Fig. 1(d)). The particle size distribution plots of CQD average diameter were ascertained through Fig. 2 (c) by examining TEM images (Fig. 1 (b)). The as prepared CQDs were dispersed with an average diameter of 2.18 ± 0.38 nm, as determined using Gaussian model (Fig. 1(c)). The observed lattice d-spacing of the lattice plane is 0.24 nm which corresponding to the (101) planes MnO2 (Fig. 1 (d and e). In Fig. 2(f) shows the SAED pattern of the hybrid sample which has an annular diffraction arrangement with bright spots which exhibit the polycrystalline nature of the prepared sample. The MWCNTs and CQDs are accountable for the polycrystalline nature of prepared samples, as evidenced by the ring-like pattern in SAED image.

FESEM images of as-prepared pure MnO2, MWCNT, and hybrid samplesare shown in Figure S1. The pure MnO2 nanoparticles have a rod-like structure with lengths ranging from 300 to 1000 nm and thicknesses ranging from ∼20–50 nm. The addition of MWCNTs and CQDs slightly altered the morphology of MnO2 rods into a highly agglomerated lamellar assembly, as shown in Figure S1 (c and d). Because of the strong electrostatic interaction between CQDs and MWCNTs, with MnO2 nanoparticles the resulting nanoparticles agglomerate. Figure S2 depicts an EDAX study of as prepared samples, which indorses that the presence of carbon, Mn, and O elements supports the formation of hybrid sample with the desired weight and atomic ratios. The element incidence and distributions of the prepared samples were evaluated using EDX andelemental mapping technique respectively. Figure S3 (a) depicts a uniformly homogeneous distribution of Mn and O elements. The, carbon, oxygen, manganese, and nitrogen elements were highly distributed in hybrid samples (Figure S3 (b & c)).

The XRD analysiswas used to investigate the crystalline nature, crystallite size, lattice d- spacing, and lattice planes over 2θ ranging from 10 to 80°. Fig. 2 depicts diffraction patterns of MnO2, MWCNT, chitosan, and different weight percentages of CQD and MWCNT assisted MnO2. In MnO2 nanoparticles, the diffraction peaks were observed at (2θ) 28.82, 37.33, 42.84, 56.72, 59.35, and 72.3° which are assigned for the planes of (011), (101), (111), (211), (220) and (112) respectively that are in strong agreement with JCPDS File no. 24-0735 (β -MnO2) [23,35]. Also, no additional impurity phases were found in the initial MnO2 nanoparticles, which indicates the presence of a high-purity β-MnO2 phase. A strong diffraction pattern found at 24.36° in Fig. 6(b) resembles the graphitic layered structure of MWCNT [36]. The XRD pattern of pure chitosan powder is shown in Fig. 2 (c), and the peaks appears at 15° and 20° demonstrate the formation of crystalline chitosan-chromone derivative [37].

Compared to the pristine MnO2, MWCNT and CQD asupported MnO2 nanoparticles exhibits a low diffraction intensity ( Fig. 2 (d) and (c)), confirming the formation of the hybrid sample. The Deby Scherrer equation was used to calculate the crystallite size of the samples. The average crystallite size of the chitosan, MWCNT, MnO2, MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-1, and MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2 are 6.8, 12.4, 76.5, 67.8, and 70.0 respectively. Also, measured lattice strain value for MnO2, MWCNT, chitosan, MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-1, and MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2 are 0.0019, 0.0126, 0.0298, 0.0022, and 0.0021, respectively.

Fig. 3 shows the FTIR spectra of prepared samples. In all spectra, major characteristic peaks appear at 3431 and 1092 cm−1 detected due to the O–H stretching vibration of surface adsorbed water molecules and aromatic C-O, respectively [38]. In Fig. 3(a), strong absorption band at 587 cm−1 is assigned to metal–oxygen stretching mode, signifying the presence of Mn-O bond in MnO2 structure [39]. A short band at 1026 cm−1 was also observed, associated with bending vibrations of OH in combination with Mn atoms [40]. In Fig. 3 (b), strong peaks detected at 1662 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1 were accredited to stretching vibration of CO and CC of carboxylic groups (COOH), respectively [41]. Functionalized MWCNTs obtained by acid treatment, which was confirmed by the presence of carboxyl groups in the FTIR spectra of MWCNTs. Hybrid samples of show sharp envelope at 3674 and 1714 cm−1 are originated due the presence of OH and CO respectively. Similarly, the CO and MO stretching vibrations were found at 1538 and 400–600 cm−1 respectively, which confirms the existence of CQD, MWCNT, and MnO2.

VU-DRS and PL analysis were used to examine the optical properties of the samples. Their observed results are shown in Figure S4. As prepared, pristine CQDs and MnO2 samples exhibit strong absorbance in UV and visible light regions. As a result of the formation of hybrid samples, the absorbance has been extended to visible light. MWCNT/CQD/MnO2−-2 exhibit their maximum absorbance peaks between 380 and 420 nm and 590 to 800 nm. It confirms that the prepared sample has a visible light absorbance profile. Also, the energy bandgaps were determined using Tauc’s plot, and the observed energy bandgaps of MnO2, MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-1, and MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2 are 3.0, 2.59 and 2.39 eV, respectively. There is a reduction in energy bandgap values, which could be caused by intermediate energy positions of CQDs and MWCNTs. The 330 nm excitation wavelength was used for all the samples in order to observe the emission profiles. The PL analysis clearly shows a decrease in emission intensity after the introduction of MWCNTs and CQDs. It is evident from the results that the hybrid sample is suitable for the photocatalysis process, which can be used for the degradation of dyes effectively 1
1 (https://doi.org/10.1002/adfm.202100816) (https://doi.org/10.1021/cs4000624) (https://doi.org/10.1007/s40820-020-00552-9).

Photocatalytic degradation capability of prepared samples was studied against the MB under the UV–Visible light irradiation. The VU-visible absorption spectrum of the MB dye in the presence of MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2 was shown in figure S5. Fig. 4 shows the C vs. C0 cure of the prepared samples here C0 and C are the initial and real time concentration of MB dye.

The observed degradation efficiency of the prepared samples was 46.52, 10.16, 18.18, 72.24 and 89.83 % for MnO2, MWCNT, Chitosan, MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-1, and MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2 respectively. The photocatalytic degradation efficiency of the pristine MWCNT and chitosan exhibits very low degradation efficiency its maybe due to the insufficient charge carrier’s generation of MWNCT and chitosan. Comparatively the pristine MnO2 exhibits considerable degradation ability against MB dye. Remarkably after the introduction of MWCNT and CQD with MnO2 the photocatalytic degradation of extensively improved. In particularly the MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2 showed an excellent degradation ability against the MB dye which may be due to the improved charge carrier mobility of the hybrid nanoparticles.

To determine the ability of the prepared samples to photocatalytic degradation, the kinetic parameters and coefficients were observed by constructing a pseudo-first order model [42]. The obtained rates of photocatalytic decomposition of the prepared samples were 0.0077, 0.0014, 0.0025, 0.0164 and 0.029 min−1 for MnO2, MWCNTs, chitosan, MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-1 and MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2, respectively, which is consistent with the results obtained by photocatalytic degradation. A high-rate constant of the reaction of the hybrid MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2 sample was observed. The observed ability of the prepared samples to photocatalytic degradation with respect to the MB dye corresponds to a pseudo first order reaction. The observed ability of the prepared samples to photocatalytic degradation with respect to the MB dye is fitted with pseudo first order reaction.

The possible MB degradation mechanism of in MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2 hybrid nanoparticles under UV–Visible light irradiation is presented in Fig. 6 . When exposed to light, MnO2 and CQDs excited electrons from valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB), leaving a hole in the VB. Moreover, electrons in CB migrated to CQDs and MWCNTs, which acted as electron carriers, accepting electrons and then donating them to react with dye molecules and destroy dyes. In the process of photocatalytic degradation of MB dye, CO2 and H2O are the main byproducts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anticancer effect of MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-1 on HeP2 cell line
ce:section.ce:para:
Figd. 8-10
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anticancer effect of MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2 on HeP2 cell line
ce:section.ce:para:
The biological properties of chitosan include immunostimulatory, anticancer, antifungal, and antibacterial properties [43-45]. Also, chitosan nanoparticles have a stronger affinity for negatively charged biological membranes, allowing them to be targeted to sites in vivo [46]. According to previous reports, carbon nanomaterials have attracted a lot of focus in image analysis and diagnostic tools due to their unique physicochemical characteristics, which make them potentially helpful for biological applications [47]. Because of their vast surface area and chemical stability, carbon nanoparticles are intriguing novel materials for use as drug nanocarriers in biomedicine, allowing efficient drug loading via both covalent and non-covalent interactions [48]. More crucially, the redox process that occurs during the catalytic path of H2O2 break in the cellular micro-environment produces a large of Mn2+, which is can be used contrast medium for magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) [49]. Thus, combining chitosan with MnO2@MWCNT have attained 50 % cell inhibition at minimal concentration due to their good synergy through effective molecular bindings. On the other hand, CQD nanoparticles may have a stronger attraction for negatively charged biomembranes, allowing for site-specific targeting in vivo [50]. CQD has a number of biological properties, including immunostimulatory, anticancer, antifungal, and antibacterial properties [43,44]. Also, the redox process that arises during the catalytic route of H2O2 break - down in cellular environment produces a large amount of Mn2+, which is used as a difference medium in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) [49]. Further, Table 2
presents anticancer activity of various carbon-based nanoparticles and its composites against wide range of cancer cell lines. Finally, Fig. 9 (a-e) and Fig. 10 (a-e) presents the anticancer effect of MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-1 and MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2 at various concentrations 1000 µg/mL, 250 µg/mL, 62.5 µg/mL and 15.6 µg/mL on HeP2 Cell line. Toxicity effects of the developed MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-1,2 were observed through changes in the morphology of Hep2 Cell line.

HeP2 cells were treated with MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-1 and MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2 nanohybrid samples and % cell viability was observed after 24 hrs (Fig. 7 and Fig. 8). The cell viability represents the number of HeP2 cells which remain alive after interacted with the prepared MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-1 and MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2. The cytotoxicity tests were performed at fixed concentration (1000, 500, 250, 125, 62.5, 31.2, 15.6, and 7.8 g/ml) of preparedsamples at a fixed incubation periods (24 hrs.) nearly half of the cancer cells were destroyed [51]. In the present study, when a maximum concentration of 1000 µg/ml of MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-1 was observed on HeP2 cell line, % cell viability is 16.27 and for NP2, % cell viability is 10.46. Likewise, when a minimum concentration of 7.8 µg/ml of MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-1 was targeted on HeP2 cell line, % cell viability is 69.76 and for NP2, % cell viability is 65.11. MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-1 have 31.2 µg/ml as IC50 concentration with cell viability as 52.32 %. Similarly, MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2 with high concentration of MWCNT have 15.6 µg/ml as IC50 concentration with % cell viability as 54.65 as reported in Table1. All these results affirm that MWCNT play a significant role on comparison with chitosan in achieving 50 % cell viability at minimum IC50 concentration.

More crucially, the redox process during the catalytic route of H2O2 breakdown the cellular microenvironments result in the generation of a huge quantity of Mn2+, which has used as a contrast agent for MRI. Thus, combining MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-1 have attained 50 % cell inhibition at minimal concentration due to their good synergy through effective molecular bindings.

In conclusion, a novel MWCNT and CQDs supported MnO2 hybrid nanoparticles were synthesized by hydrothermal method in different weight ratios. The formation of the hybrid nanoparticles, functional groups, elemental analysis and structural analysis were confirmed by various analytical instruments. The photocatalytic degradation efficiency of the as prepared samples was studied using MB dye under UV–vis. light irradiation. The optimal weight percentages of MWCNT and CQDs with MnO2 nanohybrid (MWCNT and CQDs is 0.05:0.1 g and 0.1:0.05 g) was prepared. The degradation efficiency of MB by MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-1 is 89.83 % under visible light irradiation for 75 min. Finally, a photocatalytic reaction mechanism for the removal of MB dye was proposed. The higher photocatalytic degradation and anticancer activity of the MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2 hybrid nanomaterial is attributed to the high charge carrier mobility of the hybrid samples. Similarly, the anticancer activity of the MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-1 and MWCNT/CQD/MnO2-2 was investigated against the Hep2 cell line which shows the good cell inhibition profile. Hence, the present study confirmed that the MWCNT and CQDs (0.05:0.1 g) with MnO2 nanohybrid sample can be used as a good photocatalysis as well as an anticancer agent.

K. Bhuvaneswari: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. B.S. Sreeja: Data curation, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Formal analysis. S. Radha: Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis. J. Saranya: Data curation, Investigation, Validation, Methodology. G. Palanisamy: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis. M. Srinivasan: Visualization, Data curation, Formal analysis. T. Pazhanivel: Data curation, Validation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2022.110250.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficient photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin and bisphenol A under visible light using Gd 2 WO 6 loaded ZnO/bentonite nanocomposite
